articles.title: 1932 - Telepathic Experiments Greece - New York
articles.excerpt: International telepathy experiments between Athens and New York under Professor Gardner Murphy.

content.value: Today the telepathy experiments begin with the American Society for Psychical Research under Professor Gardner Murphy, between Athens and New York, accounting for the meridian difference between the two, at 3 hours 30 minutes and 42 seconds.

The experiments will be conducted using the Carrington method, professor of Psychophysiology at the University of Cambridge, and will last throughout the winter period, every Wednesday. In total, there will be 4 each time. In the first two, the receiving group will be the American team; in the remaining 2, the Greek team, which is considered one of the best in the world.
Permanent members will be mediums who have distinguished themselves for many years in similar experiments with almost all European capitals. The ladies:

Eleni Zakynthinoy, Elpida Pavlatou, Marina and Daphne. In addition to these, a crowd of distinguished amateurs will also regularly participate: Miss Kleio, the most famous telekinetic medium, Mrs. Sivylla, Mrs. Char. Perakaki, Miss Megareios and Mr. I. Nikolaidis.
The results will be posted at the Society.

articles.title: 1959 - Tanagras' Letter: Psychophysiology and Spiritualism - Eleftheria
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Tanagras' reply to the newspaper Eleftheria regarding scientific spiritualism.

content.value: Dear Editor,

We have the honour to declare, regarding a letter published in 'Eleftheria', that what is written about Spiritualism as a science is not accurate, insofar as the phenomena supposedly created by spirits can be produced by mediums on a much larger scale, without involving spirits.
Spiritualism is a simple unproven hypothesis which has no scientific basis.

What is taught at the largest foreign universities is not spiritualism, but the latent properties within living organisms — superhuman properties such as Telepathy, Clairvoyance, Telekinesis, Psychobolia, the Akaïa of the Anastenaries, etc. Evidence of our divine origin as inseparable parts in the form of electrons and protons of some mysterious and inaccessible-to-man force, which constitutes and governs the universe, with adaptability and purposefulness, and is therefore conscious.

These superhuman phenomena tend to manifest only in states of diminished material senses (sleep - hypnosis - ecstatic states) and are taught at the largest universities, excluding our own, not as spiritualism, but as the Physiology of the Soul.
The spiritualist theory is good only as consolation for suffering individuals who naturally wish to deceive themselves.
Yours respectfully,
Angelos Tanagras.

articles.title: 1930 - The Congress of Psychophysiology - Yesterday's Announcements
articles.excerpt: Detailed account of the proceedings at the 1930 Congress of Psychophysiology with emphasis on telekinetic phenomena and telepathy.

content.value: YESTERDAY'S INTERESTING ANNOUNCEMENTS

Yesterday morning the Congress of Psychophysiology continued at 'Parnassos'. The session was chaired by Mr. K. Spentzas, professor at the University of Vienna and member of the Governing Committee of the International Psychophysiological Society of Warsaw.

First to speak was Mr. von Winter, president of the Austrian Society for Psychical Research, who presented a very interesting paper on a case of telekinesis observed at spiritualist gatherings organised at the suggestion of Professor Bisel and Dr. Studer. Many scientists attended these gatherings, and it was acknowledged as a fact that, without touch or human contact, various objects had moved.
Mr. Spentzas spoke on the same subject.
Mr. Tanagras spoke on the phenomenon of thought transference. He developed the view that the cases observed by Greek psychophysiologists must clearly be attributed to telepathy.
He further noted that in a young girl, telekinetic phenomena were observed, which gave rise to study and explanation through telepathy.
Subsequently, a scientific address was delivered by Dr. Angelos Fourdounaris, a phrenologist and pathologist in Berlin.
THE TRANSMISSION OF THOUGHT

Extensive and extremely interesting was the presentation by the president of the Polish Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Simon, in which the 'Greek Society for Psychical Research' conducted an extensive experiment in thought transmission over distances. Mr. Simon and his colleagues described the experiment in detail and explained that the transmission was clear and continuous between the two participants, even when they were at a distance of a thousand metres or in a completely dark room.

Mr. Simon stated: 'Whether we like it or not, whether it pleases us or not, one thing is now certain: that thought is transmitted, and indeed independently of distance. Experience proves it, and the fact can no longer be disputed.'

Mr. Tanagras, returning to the same subject, explained that thought transmission must be distinguished into: transmission of simple emotion, intellectual impression, aesthetic emotion, image, and others.
OTHER ANNOUNCEMENTS

Next to speak was the president of the 'Greek Society for Psychical Research', Mr. Tanagras, who noted in his presentation that the forces displayed by the 'sensitives' had already been highlighted in the announcements of the Paris Congress. That is, in a later stage, when one tries to transmit them through hypnotic means, we have the production of internal function phenomena.
The hypnotic method had no different aim, but had been specifically determined.

The speaker revealed that memories can never describe details that the individual knew or felt earlier, unless they have experienced them hallucinatorily. Mr. Tanagras explained that the phenomena attributed to hypnotists are not always real transmitted forces, but rather emotions or impressions transmitted through telepathy.

The secretary general of the Greek Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Vlastos, then spoke on the physiology of suggestion, in relation to Greece and France. He cited cases observed in France and in Greece, which presented the same interest and proved that cases of physiological suggestion can be distinguished and determined.
Mr. Vlastos finally examined all the results of physiological suggestion in sensitive people, in terms of their morals and character.
Finally, Mr. Konstantinidis spoke on the theory of the evolution of telepathy and telekinetic phenomena.
At the end of the session, Messrs. Winter, Esterkauer and Gilburg also spoke.

articles.title: Mr. Tanagras on Mediums - 1934
articles.author: Αγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: The Society for Psychical Research warns that the information provided by mediums should not always be considered as definitive truths

content.value: On the occasion of the remarkable discovery of a theft in the preceding days by a medium, the Society for Psychical Research considered it its duty to remind the public that such successes are not continuous and that the information provided by mediums should not always be considered as definitive truths.

Mediums are not machines to be wound up at any time one pleases. They are a psychological phenomenon, which is still subject to reactions we do not fully understand. Importantly, above all, in questions put to mediums, one must keep in mind the possibility of direct thought transference. If someone has a suspicion about a person and the medium repeats it, that is not proof.
Answers in general are best considered as indications for verification and as new leads to follow. Only from this perspective can mediums be beneficially used.

In this way, the day before yesterday the police confirmed the finding of a confused elderly man with amnesia by the medium Pavlatou, as well as the confirmation of a woman's death three years ago in Macedonia by the medium Cassandra.
The public is advised above all not to ask mediums for the impossible and the irrational — for example, the future or influence on absent persons. Mediums who accept such experiments are fraudsters.

It is also emphasised to the public that scientifically trained mediums self-hypnotise. Any involvement of a third party as a supposed hypnotist should be considered theatrical and suspicious. The Society is pleased to provide all information by telephone.

articles.title: Psychical Phenomena and Criminal Investigation Police
articles.author: Ν.Α Αρχιμανδρίτης - Αστυνομικός Διευθυντής Α
articles.excerpt: Lecture on the use of psychical phenomena in criminal investigation police, with emphasis on psychological analysis and lawful practices.

content.value: On 30 January 1955, at the invitation of the Greek Society for Psychical Research, a lecture was delivered in its hall by Police Director A Mr. Nikolaos Archimandritos on the subject: 'The use of psychical phenomena by criminal investigation police'.

This lecture was attended by a large audience. Thus: Ladies and gentlemen, the venerable, ever-youthful and dearest president of the Greek Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Angelos Tanagras, included me among the roster of speakers, which I could not fail to attend.
Thus, after fifteen full years, it is with great pleasure that I see so many dear faces, who have never ceased to serve science in its effort to research psychical phenomena.

The subject that will occupy us today is 'the use of psychical phenomena by criminal investigation police'. But before we address this subject, I would like to tell you a few things about our profession, that is, the profession of police officers.

You will certainly have read detective novels, which so abound in bookshops and kiosks. The Sherlock Holmeses, the Nat Pinkertons, the Dicks and the other famous detectives of those novels will have made you think that our offices are full of mysteries and subterranean tools that we use each time to discover mysterious criminals.

This, however, is not true. We are like everyone else, and for each case we use the means that sciences and arts provide us with in order to prevent various crimes or to hand over to justice those people who managed to commit a major crime without being immediately identified.

These are the various dark crimes, where police officers must act calmly, methodically, scientifically, psychologically, and without omitting anything whatsoever that could help in their elucidation and in the apprehension of the perpetrators.

Thus, these police officers must have a varied and very rich encyclopaedic education, in order to gather the elements that will lead to success. But above all they must have great psychological ability.

A police officer who cannot, in a given case, psychologically assess everyone and everything, will have very little hope of success. A good police officer must not omit any action whatsoever, in order to gather and secure the exploitation of various traces, evidence and psychological observations, which will gradually lift the veil of mystery and reveal the crime and the criminals, wherever they may be hiding.

Therefore, today it must be considered that the police officer who, in a given case, categorically rejects — and refuses to include in the scope of his investigations — the data from various psychological observations and psychical phenomena, does not have complete and full training.

From a procedural standpoint, a police officer may in his investigations for the elucidation of a crime undertake any lawful action, meaning any action that does not infringe upon the personal freedom, honour and physical integrity of citizens.
There is therefore no obstacle for the Police to use, in the investigation of a criminal case, thought transference (telepathy), clairvoyance, psychometry or other psychical phenomena.

Naturally, the elements identified through telepathy, clairvoyance or psychometry must undergo verification. And this is precisely the most delicate point of all, because the verification must be conducted scientifically and technically perfectly, so as to avoid errors that may damage the honour and reputation of some innocent person.

As I stated, the use of psychical phenomena by a police officer or investigator does not contravene the law, provided it does not infringe upon the individual and human rights of citizens; and this is not explicitly stated in the Code of Procedure as has been done for other investigative actions, namely for searches, handwriting analysis, etc.
A consequence of this is that one can find almost no written guidelines on how psychical phenomena are to be used in the official textbooks of Police Academies.

For this very reason, twenty years ago I wrote a relevant study entitled 'Crime — Hypnotism — Suggestion', where I developed in every possible detail all matters relating to suggestion while awake, to suggestions on hysterical individuals, auto-suggestions, amnesias, etc.

I also analysed matters relating to crimes committed by hypnotised individuals, to delirious behaviours during hypnosis, and to the criminal accountability of crimes committed by various hypnotised individuals and sleepwalkers.

In a separate chapter I also dealt with the very rare but very important phenomenon of double or multiple personality, which causes complete confusion in judicial authorities when a crime is involved, as well as in psycho-philosophers from the perspective of interpreting this phenomenon.
Finally, in my book I also examine the question of the use of psychical phenomena for the investigation of crimes.

Before citing certain cases in which psychical phenomena contributed to the investigation of various crimes and the apprehension of perpetrators, I will emphasise that police officers and investigators must use all the latest technical, scientific and practical means at their disposal, without omitting, when possible, the use of psychical phenomena as well.
And now I will cite some cases in which, through the use of psychical phenomena, both in foreign countries...

articles.title: The Difference Between Clairvoyance and Telepathy – The Case of Pavlos Nirvanas
articles.author: Άγνωστος
articles.excerpt: Explanation of the difference between telepathy and clairvoyance with theoretical analysis and a practical example from the case of Pavlos Nirvanas.

content.value: Those who do not carefully follow the phenomena presented in the columns of 'Police Chronicles' may not have a clear picture of the difference between telepathy and clairvoyance. The matter, however, is simple.
Telepathy: This is a kind of wireless telegraph between two brains.
Clairvoyance: It is like living X-rays, where the soul of the medium sees through matter as if it did not exist or as if it were transparent.

For example, dowsing, reading unknown words inside envelopes and diagnosing illnesses without any suspicion on the part of a doctor are pure clairvoyance. When there is suspicion or prior knowledge, then telepathy cannot be ruled out.

Case of Pavlos Nirvanas (1915): While walking in Phaleron, he suddenly thought that it had been a long time since he had been seriously ill. He also thought spontaneously: 'Bah! Why should I think of such things.'
Four to five days later he developed a serious throat infection that put his life in danger. Nirvanas' clairvoyance allowed him to perceive the impending illness, possibly through unconscious signs.

articles.title: The Curiosities of Clairvoyance - Angelos Tanagras
articles.author: Άγνωστος
articles.excerpt: Description of three cases of clairvoyance involving premonitions of illnesses, deaths and dangers, with references to psychodynamic phenomena.

content.value: The phenomena of living X-rays, that is of Clairvoyance, are far rarer than those of Telepathy. Nonetheless, the 'Society for Psychical Research' has documented some very interesting cases which explain mysterious events that seem inexplicable.

The case of the coffee merchant K. Theonis: Mr. Theonis, always 24 hours before falling ill, sees the same dream each time: a shapeless rock that grows and shrinks. At the same time he feels a weight on his chest. This episode is among the most evidential. Mr. Theonis, as is apparent in other phenomena too, is a medium, and each time he is about to fall ill this impression transforms from imagination into the image of a rock, which grows or shrinks according to the severity of the illness, while he simultaneously feels the weight on his chest. As noted in previous issues of 'Police Chronicles', there are mediums who perceive through Clairvoyance the illnesses of other people.

The case of Dimosthenis Kourtovic (1930): The director and owner of the newspaper 'Kiryx' of Volos, Dimosthenis Kourtovic, had been bedridden for months, but, despite repeated crises, always avoided death. On 4 April 1930, at approximately 6 to 6:30 p.m., his wife, who had briefly withdrawn from the bedroom, found him sitting up in his bed, ready to leave. She simultaneously heard him say urgently: 'Bring me my clothes quickly so I can dress... I want to go out... Giannis Tsagkaris told me to come to his office immediately. I must not be late!'

His wife calmed him and laid him down again. The next day she was dumbstruck when she was told, before even opening the newspaper, that Giannis Tsagkaris had been murdered in Thessaloniki at the very hour when the exchange between husband and wife was taking place. Four days later, Kourtovic died peacefully, without a prior crisis.

In the same case, the wife noted that many days earlier, while she was by his bedside, she heard him arguing with someone, shouting: 'Get away from here! It's not time yet! Go away!' When she asked him who it was, he replied: 'The gravedigger... I sent him away because it wasn't time yet.'

Here we observe three phenomena: Telepathy between the dying man and those who knew of the murder of Tsagkaris. Intense auto-suggestion on his part, which likely caused his death through cardiac arrest. Clairvoyance, which made him aware of the impending situation.

The case of grandfather G. Pitykakis: The grandfather, originally from Thrapsano of Heraklion, had gone to Apokorona to manufacture storage jars. The clay is extracted from underground tunnels, which sometimes reach depths of 15-20 metres. One summer midday, while sleeping at the bottom of such a tunnel for the coolness, he heard the voice of his mother, who had died many years before, calling him: 'Giannis! Get up and go outside!'

At first he thought it was an ordinary dream and turned over. But before he fell asleep again, he heard the voice more intensely: 'Get up, quickly, and go outside!' Frightened, he got up, made the sign of the cross and went outside. Within seconds, a massive mass of earth collapsed and filled the tunnel, which would most likely have killed him had he remained inside. Despite being educated and without prejudice, his imagination conveyed the clairvoyant warning as the voice of his deceased mother.

Conclusion: Clairvoyance, beyond Telepathy, demonstrates a superhuman property of psychodynamism. The psychic emission penetrates matter as if it were a separate rational and conscious entity, limited only to its sphere of action. The image formed returns to the brain, like a rational servant returning to inform his master. This constitutes strong evidence for the existence of the soul and its functioning beyond the known senses.

articles.title: What is Telepathic Suggestion - Angelos Tanagras
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Examination of the rare phenomenon of telepathic suggestion through experiments and real cases recorded by the Society for Psychical Research.

content.value: In the previous issue of 'Chronicles' we reported the case of a telepathic suggestion that occurred to the wife of physician I. Papakonstantinidis. The woman dreamed of an unsuccessful murder attempt on her husband by Turks, in Philadelphia in Asia Minor. In this article we examine the phenomenon of telepathic suggestion, which is very rare and difficult to understand, especially when attempted voluntarily and experimentally.

The 'Society for Psychical Research' conducted experiments with mediums to prove the impossibility of voluntary telepathic suggestion. Three to four mediums were hypnotised in a common room and asked to strongly think that they would suggest to another colleague the performance of an act, e.g. to take a handkerchief from the researcher's pocket. Despite intense concentration for five to ten minutes, no medium managed to carry out the suggestion. The experiments were repeated several times and proved that such suggestions are practically impossible voluntarily.

Telepathic suggestion is often exploited for deception, as fraudsters promise to influence the feelings of third parties from a distance. One example is a self-proclaimed sorceress who promised an abandoned woman that she would force the unfaithful man to pass beneath her balcony and sing a favourite song. Eventually it happened, and the sorceress' reputation grew along with her earnings.
Chief physician K. Papathanasiou submitted two incidents showing telepathic suggestion and unconscious reception of impressions.

Case 1 – June 1916, Pyrgos Elis: A close relative of Papathanasiou had departed for convalescence to the village of Voulgareli in Arta. Papathanasiou did not know the village. One night he dreamed the whole area: forest, square, church, house, rooms, veranda, vine, the grocery shop and the relative himself. A month later, when he visited Voulgareli, everything was exactly as in the dream. This proves that Papathanasiou unconsciously received the impressions of his relative, through long cohabitation and emotional connection.

Case 2 – Spring 1920, Asia Minor: Papathanasiou dreamed of Adrianople about a month before its capture by the Greek army. He saw the city panoramically: mosques, districts, villages, the Evros river. He had never seen the city in print. When he visited the city in August 1921, it was exactly as he had seen it in the dream. In this case, there was no known transmitter — the telepathic suggestion occurred unconsciously, through the synchronisation of psychic waves.

Telepathic suggestion compels the receiver to unconsciously execute the acts, words or positions that appear in the dream. A rough proof is that in public gatherings we can force people who have their backs to us to turn around by persistent focusing, or when we feel we are being observed, we turn and see someone watching us.

Conclusion: Telepathic suggestion is a rare phenomenon that is activated unconsciously and cannot be imposed voluntarily. The recording of these incidents by the 'Society for Psychical Research' constitutes important testimony for the research of psychical phenomena.

articles.title: Various Forms of Telepathy - Angelos Tanagras
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Analysis of various cases of telepathy with representational and notifying characteristics, recorded by the Society for Psychical Research.

content.value: Mr. Epam. G. Garmpis dreamed, on 22 November 1926, that his brother Iraklis, who was in Rio de Janeiro, Argentina, was walking with a procession towards the cemetery. He woke up and when he fell asleep again he saw the same man returning from the cemetery to his home. The dream worried him, and he waited for some bad news, because he had observed that his dreams often came true. Indeed, the following December he received a letter from his brother announcing the death of his son Friderikos, which occurred exactly on 22 November, the day of the dream, after a long illness of which Garmpis however knew nothing.

He also had, years before, an unfortunate relative whom he was following, and one evening he saw him in a dream rosy-cheeked, contented, saying: 'Epameinonda! I have rested. I am content.' Two days later he learned that the relative had indeed died the same day and had been buried by neighbours who did not know his address.

The first case of Garmpis is pure representational telepathy, where the image of his brother heading to and returning from the cemetery was transmitted from the sender. In this telepathy there is also an apparent symbolism of the subconscious, which attempts to indicate to the receiver's reason the misfortune.
The second case is notifying-form telepathy, which actively conveys the release of a tormented man for whom life was a martyrdom.

Anna Becker dreamed in 1912 of her brother, with whom she had a strong affinity, standing naked. She was alarmed by the vividness of the dream and noted the date, recounting the dream to her circle. Two weeks later she received a letter from her father in Alexandria announcing that her brother had died exactly the same day in Barcelona, Spain. She later learned that death had resulted from a stroke as he was coming out of the bath. In this case we again have representational-form telepathy: the subconscious could not transmit the entire image of the accident, but only one aspect of it — the deceased standing and naked, as he would have appeared coming out of the bath.

Another representational-form telepathy was observed in A. Papadiamantopoulos, who was about to depart on 11 August 1928 for Iarissus due to his seriously ill brother Konstantinos. That evening he dreamed of an intense flash of light, within which he distinguished a patient's room and a woman walking inside it. He woke up and noted the time: 4:20 a.m. He had a strong feeling that his brother had died, that the room was his, and that the woman walking inside was his wife. The impression was so strong that he told his mother and his brother-in-law Papadopoulos: 'Why should we go to Iarissus! That man is dead.' Indeed, arriving in Patras, they received a telegram: 'Mort hier matin' (died yesterday morning). Papadiamantopoulos was informed that death had occurred exactly at 4:20 a.m.

Mr. I. Lampropoulos, a retired Gendarmerie officer with strong intuitive properties, had a pending case at the Court of First Instance. One evening he dreamed that he was handed a white legal document with his name on it. He saw it puzzled and when he woke up had the feeling that a favourable decision had been issued in his favour, and he noted the date on the wall. He was so convinced of the favourable decision that he did not even pass by the Court of First Instance to learn the result. Two months later, being called for another matter at the Court, his brother urged him to stop for information. When he showed the dream and the note of the date, the other shook his head sceptically. Eventually, he learned that the decision on his case had been issued favourably, exactly on the date he had noted.

articles.title: Psychometric Phenomena - Angelos Tanagras
articles.author: Άγνωστος
articles.excerpt: Exploration of the phenomenon of psychometry — the ability to read the past through objects that carry the psychic emissions of the people who touched them.

content.value: 'The past does not lie latent in the present, but lives in it.' Every event leaves its imprint in the environment. Even the strap of a dead soldier can reveal his entire history.

Having described two of the most important phenomena of psychophysiology, telepathy and clairvoyance, as well as those relating to suggestion, we arrive at the third manifestation of the soul's properties, known as psychometry. From very long ago, popular experience observed that various seers or mediums answered questions about certain individuals much better and more reliably if they held objects belonging to them, such as handkerchiefs, clothes, watches or rings, and especially organic elements of their body, such as hair or nails.

Repeating similar experiments in America, physician Buchanan, around the middle of the previous century, gave mediums letters from persons unknown to them to handle. He observed that, by applying the contact to the forehead, mediums could describe the character and life of the writer far better than his close friends. After continuing the experiments for a long time with the same result, he wondered how the phenomenon occurred.

Buchanan accepted the first hypothesis and named, perhaps unfortunately, the phenomenon 'Psychometry'. Astonished by this aspect of the mysterious unknown being revealed, he wrote: 'The past lies latent in the present. The discoveries of psychometry will permit the exploration of human history, just as geology allows us to determine the history of the earth.'

Later, geologist Denton, whose wife was a medium, through a series of experiments, completed the theory, arguing that 'the past does not lie latent in the present, but lives in it, so that the observer can describe it as if he had been present when it occurred.' The medium becomes the embodiment of events and their protagonist.

In the Greek Society for Psychical Research the experiment is conducted as follows: since not all mediums are receptive to suggestion, they are given a simple piece of paper to hold for a few minutes so that it becomes imbued with their psychodynamism. It is then marked with a small imperceptible sign, mixed with other similar papers, and given to the hypnotised medium. They must then find the paper they imbued, and indeed find it with 90–100% success.

The experiment was repeated regularly at the weekly sessions of the Society for Psychical Research, owing to its great importance for criminology. Entirely exceptional too is the experiment where a magnetised crown was placed on the head of a patient with melancholic psychosis, achieving her cure within six sessions.

Denton in his book 'The Soul of Things' emphasised: 'Every intensity of human emission, such as great sorrow, strong fear, vivid joy or any intellectual activity, strengthens the impregnation (invocation of psychic energy) of objects. This means that when we are in a state of psychic or intellectual intensity, we more powerfully impregnate our surroundings and the objects we carry.'

In 1919, physician Riesenstern, discovering in Mexico an exceptional medium, Maria Reyes, observed that she did not merely see a cinematic film of events, but embodied them, as if she herself were the protagonist. As she said: 'All my senses function. I see, I feel, I taste, I perceive the cold or the heat, I suffocate. In short, I am as if present in all the scenes.'

An artificial flower petal from a church statue, a strap from a dead soldier, various historical relics, gave the medium the opportunity to make descriptions of astonishing accuracy. Letters written by persons unknown to the medium she fully reproduced, while when given a plain sheet of paper, she described only the structure of the manufacturing factory.

There was already a suspicion that this might be telepathic thought transference from persons who knew the events, but this was excluded by strict experiments of examination committees. The medium, for example, felt in a piece of stone that had remained for three weeks near a clock the rhythmic ticking; in another piece impregnated with saccharine, a sweet taste.

The author had the opportunity to repeat analogous psychometric experiments at the Society for Psychical Research in Greece. The difficulty is great, as care is required to avoid confusion with phenomena of telepathy and clairvoyance, from which many had benefited.

articles.title: The Effect of Suggestion on Pregnant Women - Angelos Tanagras
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Description of the phenomenon of suggestion's effect on pregnant women and how intense impressions can influence the fetus, with historical and medical examples.

content.value: The tremendous power of suggestion in sensitive and nervous individuals gives rise to a particularly interesting phenomenon, which popular experience had observed long ago. This is the scientific 'stigmatic ideoplasty of pregnant women'.

It was observed that many women, especially those who had suffered or been sensitive during their pregnancy, when they saw something that made a strong impression on them, could imprint that image on the fetus.

The ancient Greeks were aware of this phenomenon and decorated the homes of pregnant women with beautiful statues, so that their children would be born beautiful. From this derives, in part, the so-called 'national type' of various peoples. In parallel, famous men whose likenesses were disseminated in coins and statues transmitted their physiognomies to a wide public. The Macedonians, for example, still today retain characteristics of Alexander the Great.

In the Cyclades and among the Greek communities of Asia Minor the tradition of the influence of objects and images on pregnant women is preserved to this day. The Smyrnaean scholar Angelos Drosos noted that during weddings in Smyrna, images with tragic scenes were replaced with images of beautiful women and cherubs, so that the children would resemble the ideal types.

Examples from medicine: The Dutch physician van Swieten attempted to remove a caterpillar from the neck of a young pregnant woman. The woman, laughing, said she would bring the caterpillar with her into the life of her child. The physician observed that the caterpillar 'embodied' its shape, colour and hairs in the child.

Physician Bratis found a child with a scar on its upper lip, the result of a surgical operation the mother had witnessed during her pregnancy. The impression was so intense that the child was born with exactly the same scar, without having undergone surgery.
A German journal of 1918 describes a pregnant woman who saw a bat land on her shoulder. The child was born with a faithful depiction of the bat on its body, with correct colour, nails and snout.

Paracelsus knew that the imagination of pregnant women can influence the fetus in many ways. Van Helmont emphasised that a pregnant woman who intensely desires something, e.g. a cherry, can imprint its shape on the body of the child, even at the point where she was touching it at the moment of desire.

Parallel with animals: The cuckoo's behaviour regarding the eggs of other birds shows a similar effect. The bird adapts the colour of its eggs precisely to the appearance of the eggs of the foreign nests, so that the parents do not suspect the deception. An analogous phenomenon is observed in the Papilio Machaon chrysalis, which takes on the colour of the place where it approaches for pupation, as well as in insects, spiders, reptiles, fish, birds and mammals.

The fundamental conclusion is that the power of idea and imagination can influence not only the organism of the pregnant woman but also the fetus or eggs, proving the importance of suggestion in the progress and formation of life.

articles.title: The Exploiters of Psychical Phenomena
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Text by the Society of Psychophysiology warning the public about fraudsters who exploit the credulity and pain of people, presenting methods of deception and proposals for the protection of citizens from pseudo-mediums, pseudo-magicians and pseudo-healers.

content.value: The Society of Psychophysiology, with its thirty years of experience, warns of the unchecked activity of pseudo-magicians, pseudo-healers and pseudo-mediums, who exploit the credulity of the suffering and afflicted.

The fraudsters promise: the dissolution of spells, telepathic interventions on absent persons, healing of illnesses in exchange for high fees, black magic to cause harm to third parties, or predictions of the future.
They often invoke fake titles, non-existent certifications or associations without any scientific basis, which believe they communicate with the spirits of ancient philosophers.
The Society of Psychophysiology notes that these practices are frauds and must be dealt with severely.
For those who promise to dissolve spells (fraud no. 1), the penal law on Sorcery applies.
For those who claim telepathic imposition on absent persons (fraud no. 2), this must be considered fraud, as even at close range it is a rare phenomenon.
For the healers (fraud no. 3), referral to hospitals for examination under scientific supervision is proposed.

For Black Magic (fraud no. 4), perpetrators fall within the law and moreover society must be protected even more strictly, owing to the existence of 'psychobolous' individuals who can scientifically cause harm.

For fake certificates (fraud no. 5), the creation of an official committee of specialist psychiatrists, with university participation, is proposed, so that mediums may be examined and institutionalised certification issued.
The publication in the Press of unverified phenomena must be strictly prohibited, while the police should confiscate misleading publications.
Recognised and genuine mediums must be protected by the state, but their answers must be considered only as indications and not as evidence.
For future-telling predictions (fraud no. 6), it is considered that severity must apply, as they have even caused deaths through auto-suggestion.
Finally, spiritualist associations may continue their gatherings on the condition that they have no exploitative purpose.

articles.title: The Cure of Love Through Suggestion
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Two cases of 'stigmatic self-wounding' — Love as intense auto-suggestion — Hypnotic suggestion, the only cure for the lovesick

content.value: Two cases of 'stigmatic self-wounding' — Love as intense auto-suggestion — Hypnotic suggestion, the only cure for the lovesick
Physician Panagiotis P. Panagiotou recounts the following incident:
'My contact with the 'Society for Psychical Research' gave me the occasion to explain an event that until then had remained inexplicable to me.

I was seven years old at the time. I remember we were at the fair of the Taxiarchis, in a village near Keratia in Attica, where I had gone with an acquaintance. When we returned, I had bought some things, mainly sweets for the house, of which, however, I gave nothing to my brother.
This upset my mother, who scolded me, telling me that I should not have done it, because 'the Holy Taxiarchis will punish me.'
I had forgotten the event when I fell asleep. But in my sleep I saw three gendarmes, one of whom cut a switch from a fir tree and began beating me on the shins.

In the morning when I got up, I indeed saw my shins bruised and with visible marks of blows. My mother naturally attributed it to the wrath of the Taxiarchis. Today, however, I can say with certainty that it was a case of 'stigmatic self-wounding'.'

Another characteristic incident occurred at a monastery in the Italian Foccamontana. There a 25-year-old carpenter, Pietro Cianci, was called to repair a wooden cross on which was nailed a Christ made of compressed paper.

The carpenter completed the repair, but when he wanted to nail the Crucified One back on, he felt his nerves paralysed. He had the impression that the act of nailing would constitute a repetition of the martyrdom and that he himself would be repeating what the Jews did to Christ.
He was barely persuaded to finish the work, but that night he was tormented by terrible dreams and in the morning woke with wounds on his hands and feet — the stigmata of the Passion.
The event spread immediately and crowds hurried to see him, including representatives of the Authorities and Science, who all confirmed the truth of the incident.
The explanation, as in all similar phenomena, is auto-suggestion, that is the effect of idea upon matter. The intense impression was what caused the dermal alterations in the carpenter.
Love as auto-suggestion
One of the most powerful and truly formidable forms of auto-suggestion, with ruinous results in sensitive organisms, is the so-called great romantic feelings.
We have developed in previous issues of 'Police Chronicles' the tremendous power of suggestion and auto-suggestion, with various characteristic examples.

Love can begin either from the impression of the 'conventionally beautiful' — since perceptions of beauty differ greatly depending on latitude and the development of the individual — or from calculations (dowry, kinship).
In individuals prone to suggestion it can end in intense auto-suggestion, which must be considered a genuine morbid symptom: ideopathy.
This ideopathy can develop under the influence of selfishness or the instinct of ownership (jealousy), even in old feelings that had waned.

Old relationships that had ended in indifference, whether due to character or physical imperfections, can flare up unilaterally and lead to ideopathy, due to a sudden wound to one's ego or fear of separation from the other person.
Part of the 'fair sex' knows this mechanism very well and handles it with great skill, systematically provoking the jealousy of their partners to keep them close.
For the romantic ideopath, the object of auto-suggestion acquires exaggerated, almost absolute value. The need for the person's presence becomes compulsive, leading to irrational acts.
Memories and the places of shared life cause deep pain. Continuous and often violent scenes of confrontation do not calm the situation but worsen it.

The ideopath revolves constantly around his obsessive idea, feels an irresistible need to speak of the beloved person and is in continuous agitation, which ends in genuine anxiety, the most painful form of psychopathy.

It manifests as a weight on the chest, palpitations, anguished restlessness, loss of appetite and sleep, leading to exhaustion and, in sensitive individuals, preparing the ground for more serious mental disorders.
The crises can become so intense as to override every other feeling and lead to crime or suicide.
Very few other forms of auto-suggestion, apart from the romantic, have such dramatic results.
The cure
Intense romantic suggestion creates in susceptible individuals a dreadful, genuine condition, in which the psychiatrist will inevitably be called to intervene.
First, anti-neurotic and antispasmodic drugs, though only auxiliary, are useful for anxiety attacks.
Treatment, however, since the body is not suffering, must target the soul.
Unfortunately, in these situations no logic and no persuasion can be effective.
Significant effect can come from temporary removal from the environment and memories of the auto-suggestion, with continuous change of impressions (travel) as a distraction.
Popular experience has also underlined the importance of 'homoeopathy': 'one snake eats the other' or 'one devil drives away the other.'
Thus, it has been observed that even physical engagement with another woman can have positive effects on the sufferer.

However, suitable for such occasions are not common women who sell their charms, but particular natures who can understand the therapeutic role, combining exterior qualities with rare delicacy and charm.
Where a common courtesan would fail, a woman of superior psychic power can fully succeed.

Physical approach can be considered one of the most powerful therapeutic means, as the sufferer in those moments experiences a kind of consoling illusion, confusing reality with the person of his auto-suggestion.
The only truly safe 'remedy' against such conditions, however, is hypnotic suggestion.
Unfortunately, this can be applied only to individuals who are hypnotisable and receptive to suggestion.
If we consider that approximately 20% of people are hypnotisable, only half of these accept suggestion to a greater or lesser degree.
For this reason, more will be said in the next issue.

articles.title: Astonishing Cases of Psychometry
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: What is the 'zopyron'. The creaking beam that warns of… dollars arriving! How we can impregnate surrounding objects with psychic energy. The Epipsychidion.

content.value: What is the 'zopyron'. The creaking beam that warns of… dollars arriving! How we can impregnate surrounding objects with psychic energy. The Epipsychidion.
The well-known author Nikolaos Laskaris submitted the following to the Society for Psychical Research:

When he was in Paris, he went to a medium together with his friend from Thessaloniki, Michalis Dimitriadis, who wanted to check on a letter containing money that he had been waiting for in vain for some time.
— 'You are joking, sir…', the medium told him, 'the letter you are thinking of, along with the money, is in your pocket.'
Astonished, Laskaris put his hand in his pocket and indeed found the letter sealed and intact, with the expected money order.
What had happened?

The letter had arrived about a month earlier, but the maidservant who received it, not finding Dimitriadis in his room, thought to put it in the pocket of his overcoat, forgotten, without notifying him.
Given that it is impossible that telepathic communication occurred between the maidservant and the medium, the letter acted as a 'zopyron', awakening in the latter the gift of psychometry.
The distinction between Telepathy and Psychometry is sometimes difficult, as the following example shows:
One of the best-known members of Athenian society, the late P. Kanakis, Romanian consul, once conversed with a lady who expressed skepticism about this kind of phenomenon.
That same night, around six in the morning, he saw the same lady giving him a cylinder containing 28,000 drachmas.
In the morning he received exactly the same sum by post from Romania.
In this case, the proximity of the letter likely acted as a 'zopyron' in the sleeping person's subconscious, symbolically representing the event with the skeptical lady as the protagonist.
Also not excluded is the possibility of telepathy from the sender, who may have thought of the letter around six in the morning.
Another beautiful phenomenon of psychometry was observed during my stay in Kastoria, thanks to the zeal and interest of attorney D. Tzimas:
The villager Vasileios K. Mylonas, who had children settled in America, every time he was expecting a letter that usually contained a dollar cheque, heard the beam of his house creak.
From the regular repetition of the phenomenon he became convinced of the causal connection with the arrival of the letter, and was never disappointed.

On 21 October 1924, when he had to pay 1,000 dollars, he asked attorney D. Tzimas to take no action against him, because, as always happens when a letter arrives, the beam creaks and he was inevitably expecting money.
Those present laughed, but within the day the foretold cheque indeed arrived.
From the explanation of this phenomenon, we cannot exclude a possible telekinetic effect of the old man on the roof of his house, as occurs in people with telekinetic abilities.
Another related incident was sent to our offices by the widow Aik. Markopoulou, from Sitia:
'One month after the death of my husband Ioannis Markopoulou, I saw him in my sleep telling me that beneath the sofa, where we keep the jar with wheat, his money was hidden.'
'He advised me to remove wheat with the pinakero and put my hand in, and I would find the little bag with the coins.'
In the morning, indeed, I did as he told me in the dream and found the little bag with the money there.
Also, many times my husband foretold various things concerning the family, which always proved true.
These phenomena reinforce the experimental results of psychometry, where it is proven that we impregnate objects we use with something of our psychic energy, which we have named the 'Epipsychidion'.
Has not folk experience with its intense intuition observed and verified long before Professor Buchanan the phenomenon of psychometry?

Always, intuitive individuals (sorcerers, witches) searched for something belonging to the person about whom they were inquiring — e.g., a handkerchief, a dress, a ring, etc. — especially organic remains such as hair, nails, etc.
Their experience showed that they could more easily intuit matters relating to the person when they touched objects impregnated with his psychodynamism, with his 'Epipsychidion'.
It is not only this observation that confirms the phenomenon of impregnated matter.

People at all latitudes often observed that the dying, whose loved ones were absent and who felt the loss heavily, showed in their final moments an exceptional prolongation of the death agony, and their anguish sometimes lasted an entire day.

It was then observed that the end was hastened and the dying person was released, if at that moment a garment or object belonging to the absent person was placed near them — that is, something impregnated with their Epipsychidion.
It seems that this object intervened in the darkened mental state of the dying and gave them the illusion that it was the person themselves.
This consolation led to the resolution of the obsessive idea that stimulates the half-narcotized cells, and thus death ensued.
Sometimes the same result is observed if a photograph of the absent person is placed near the patient.
This result stems no longer from the Epipsychidion, but from Clairvoyance.

The patient, subconsciously perceiving through the matter — like a form of living X-rays — the image, imagines in his stupor that it is the person themselves, and thus the same liberating result ensues.

articles.title: Do the Dead Speak, or the Soul of the Living?
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: — What is the 'necrobiosis' of memories. — The coal miner who suddenly became a painter. — The explanation of the phenomenon —

content.value: — What is the 'necrobiosis' of memories. — The coal miner who suddenly became a painter. — The explanation of the phenomenon —
The subconscious plays many games, and those less experienced in such tricks fare badly.

The particular suffering is to fall into dogmatism and imagine that the phenomena they observe do not originate from the medium's soul but from the spirits of the dead — that is, to become spiritualists. Moreover, if you consider that most people have some degree of mysticism, you can understand how easy it is to make this 'slip'.
Imagine someone with no knowledge of Telepathy going to a medium who has grown accustomed to thinking that everything it says originates from thought-transfer and is announced by spirits.
It begins thus:
— 'I see a spirit behind you! It is your late grandmother!' (Supposedly the deceased is in the eternal abodes.)
The listener is then moved but remains skeptical.
— 'Very well. If it is my grandmother, let her tell me what she was called, when she died and from what she died.'
The medium then picks up the thought of the person himself from the 'beyond', and informs him that she was called Argyro and that she died of pneumonia on such a date of December in such a year.
The listener is then dumbfounded.
— 'For the medium to tell me these details, it must be my grandmother's spirit.'
From that moment you have lost him! He has become a fanatical spiritualist and does not want to hear the truth.
Yet he simply drowned in a glass not of water, but of Telepathy.
The soul of the medium — the famous 'Subconscious' of science, or the 'Entelechy' as Aristotle called it — plays, however, other and worse tricks.
To imagine these, one must consider what Physics tells us. It tells us that everything in creation consists of electrons — that is, of energy.
Everything, therefore, including ourselves, is part of this creative energy — that is, of the Divine.
We must then possess, at least in latent form, superhuman properties. And we do, although our material condensation and the energy of reason bind them in iron chains.
We are like beings enclosed headfirst in a sack, from which we try to gain some idea of the outside world through five imperfect senses that, instead of presenting us with the real truth, distort it.
Einstein's theory of relativity applies to everything. All our perceptions are relative.

Always, however, the superhuman properties remain within us, and as soon as we manage to reduce consciousness — either naturally through sleep, or artificially through hypnotism — we see Telepathy, Clairvoyance, Psychometry, Psychobolia, etc. manifesting.
The worst thing in this material bondage is that it imprints on the subconscious the most vivid impressions of our material life.
Suddenly we see children resembling either their parents, or their grandparents (transcendent heredity), or older relatives.
They resemble to such an astonishing degree that suddenly a mole appears in the same spot on the child, or baldness begins from the same point where it began in the father, etc.
Even gestures, postures, and talents such as mathematics or music are transmitted exactly from generation to generation.
But reproductive cells are, as we know, microscopic.
What tremendous mnemonic power must they possess to transmit such similar details?
And the natural question immediately follows:
If such minute details are perpetuated, why should not knowledge, linguistic abilities, and life memories be perpetuated as well?

Perhaps they remain in a latent state, simply sleeping within the cells, and as soon as the individual finds themselves in a favorable state of reduced consciousness, they manifest and give us the impression that they come from the dead?
And so it indeed happens!

In this way it is explained that illiterate mediums sometimes speak in a hypnotic state using Byzantine or archaic phraseology, or speak dead languages that their ancestors would have spoken and whose memory has remained dormant within them.
A noteworthy similar incident of 'necrobiosis of memories' was recorded a few years ago in France.
A coal miner from the northern provinces, engaged in the rhythmic digging of his gallery, fell into a light reverie (reduction of consciousness), when he heard a voice in his ear:
— 'You will become a painter!'
Astonished, he looked around without seeing anyone.
He continued his work and heard the voice again.
That evening he recounted the phenomenon to his friends, among whom some were spiritualists.
These called him to a table-turning session and imagined that some painter was reincarnated within him.
He then took up crayons and paints and indeed began to draw with astonishing ease the most complex architectural designs.
Then his imagination began to work and he signed these drawings now as Rembrandt, now as Van Dyck, now as Velázquez, etc.
The event caused a sensation throughout France, the spiritualists celebrated, claiming that the question of reincarnation had now been verified, and an exhibition of his works was organized in Paris.
I also visited this exhibition at the Metapsychic Institute on Avenue Niel.
It was indeed something to admire — the complex, imaginative, and precise drawings of the uneducated miner, who worked in a kind of light hypnotic state.
There was, however, one detail that provided the solution to the problem.
In these paintings there were only architectural drawings.
When he tried to paint people, he was as clumsy as a novice pupil.
Therefore the legend of Rembrandt and Van Dyck collapsed automatically.
It was simply a matter of heredity.
There must have been some ancestor who was an architect or draughtsman, whose latent memories awakened in the light hypnotic state into which the miner fell during his work.
They claimed to have investigated his genealogy up to seven generations without finding any such ancestor.
It is, however, obvious that they counted without the innkeeper.

For no one can exclude the possibility that some great-grandmother of his did not fall into some pleasant …transgression with some travelling draughtsman, who 'departed' centuries later into the cells of his descendant…
Thus a thousand and one similar strange cases of hypnotized mediums are explained.

articles.title: Autosuggestion Can Cause Death!
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: The case of Captain D. Berotis, who died following a powerful suggestion, and other astonishing cases of minor accidents as a result of suggestion, demonstrate the danger of predictions.

content.value: The case of Captain D. Berotis, who died following a powerful suggestion, and other astonishing cases of minor accidents as a result of suggestion, demonstrate the danger of predictions.

The phenomena of suggestion, which show that they can have such a terrible effect on healthy people, one can imagine what significance they hold for the sick, the dying, or simply exhausted organisms.

For this reason, when it concerns individuals susceptible to suggestion, a chance idea — whether following a dream, or due to hallucinations arising from hypnotic beliefs, or simply from similar examples — can produce fatal results.
Many times dying people have died at precisely the hour they imagined, as a result of suggestion, which caused them cardiac paralysis.
We cite a few relevant examples:
The late naval captain Dim. Beratis, on the day of his death, asked his son in the morning whether it was twelve o'clock.
The latter replied that it was only half past seven.
A little later he asked again and received the answer again that it was eight o'clock.
He continued, however, until noon, while still in full possession of his senses, to address the same question to various people present, including Antonios Vlassopoulos.
At exactly twelve o'clock he departed this life.

The dying man had undergone a powerful autosuggestion that he would die at that hour, and this caused his end through cardiac arrest or cerebral haemorrhage, given that four months earlier he had suffered a hemiplegia.
A similar incident occurred with the former chief worker of 'Estia', Konst. Sifnaios, in November 1924.
While bedridden for one and a half months, he suddenly began asking when the feast of the Taxiarches was.
They answered that it had already passed, but he insisted.
— 'You don't understand me!' he said, and shortly after repeated the same question adding that he meant when it was according to the Old Calendar.
On the day of the Taxiarches he began asking from 3 in the morning whether it was eight o'clock, and continued until exactly eight, at which point he died.
The physician Dim. Detsikasalso reported the following case:
The daughter of Hippocrates Papageorgiou, Elli, aged 17, in September 1927 was gravely ill from typhoid fever.
She was recounting to her stepmother that she had seen in a dream her mother, who told her she would come to take her when she turned 17.
From this she concluded that she would die now that she had turned 17.
Indeed, a few days later, a serious intestinal haemorrhage caused her death.
Most likely, the vivid autosuggestion caused the death.
The physician Dionysios Katsikopoulos testified:
His wife's brother, Andreas Agapitos, suffering from pancreatic sclerosis, was being treated by him.
Five days before his death, on a Saturday when his condition was serious, he said:
— 'Don't worry, I shall not die today, but on Thursday…'
On that day, although his condition showed no signs of imminent danger, Katsikopoulos gave him camphor injections and serum.
In vain, for at 6 p.m. he closed his eyes and died.
The physician N. Fistas from Florina testified:
An uncle of his, now over eighty, was dying from a chronic heart condition.
His daughter was distressed because he would not be present at her wedding, which was to take place soon.
Suddenly the dying man opened his eyes and said:
— 'My daughter, do not worry! I shall die one week after your wedding…'
Indeed, three weeks later the wedding took place and on the eighth day after it the old man died.
Naturally, all these episodes cannot be explained by fate, but simply by intense autosuggestion in exhausted organisms.
Since autosuggestion can have fatal results, it can much more easily cause other minor accidents, which at first glance create the impression of fate.
The orthopaedic physician Mich. Chrysafis testified:
A patient with ankylosis of the right knee, from an old white tumour, had been put in a plaster cast by physician Serdaris at the Geroulanos clinic.
One day, the patient recounted that she had dreamed she was again at the same clinic and Serdaris was putting a new plaster cast on her.
Ten days later, while leaving a cinema, she fell and broke the femur of the affected leg.
She was transferred to the Geroulanos clinic and two days later a plaster cast was applied to her.
In this case it is not excluded that the autosuggestion caused the stumble and the fall.
A similar incident was confirmed by physician I. Panagiotou, in which the autosuggestion caused a fall from a roof, either due to stumbling or following dizziness.
A nurse at the Athens Polyclinic (Alevizatos) had predicted to sub-lieutenant Dim. Karelan that in 1927 he would be promoted, but shortly afterwards he would die.
The sub-lieutenant did not give credence to the prediction, because he was not due for promotion.
However, changes were indeed made to the Seniority List, Karelas was unexpectedly promoted and posted to Larissa.
From that point he underwent a powerful autosuggestion, fearing lest the second prediction also come true.
That same month, December, while hunting a turtledove, he saw it fall on the roof of an aerodrome and climbed up to catch it.
There, however, he slipped, fell, and died.
This episode demonstrates how disastrously predictions can affect people in general, when they have some basis of plausibility.

In this case the nurse appears to have had intuitive properties and to have perceived telepathically from individuals at the Ministry of Military Affairs the forthcoming changes to the Seniority List and Karelas's promotion.
Her imagination, however, wove by chance or following a strong impression similar accounts and a prediction of death.

The realization of the promotion produced a powerful autosuggestion in Karelas, the result of which was the stumble or dizziness, as soon as he found himself in a dangerous situation (climbing onto a roof).
For this reason we point out repeatedly that it is extremely dangerous to seek predictions, unless intuitive individuals can systematically refine them.
ANGELOS TANAGRAS

articles.title: Telepathy Between Diseased Brains
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Representational Telepathy of veterinarian D. Dimosthenous — How physician Lorandos inadvertently suggested — The strange case of former ambassador A. Papadiamantopoulos

content.value: The military veterinarian Dimosthenis Dimosthenous, during the mobilization of 1915, was stationed in the village of Peteresko, near Sorovic, and was sleeping on the upper floor of a village house.
On the lower floor slept villagers and his groom.

At about 3 in the morning he dreamed of his friend, university professor Louros, who told him that his brother, a professor at the Conservatory, suffering from a pulmonary disease, supposedly needed to have an operation, and moreover the operation had to be performed on the roof of the house, where the air was clean.
Dimosthenous, still in his dream, accepted the proposal and saw the two of them on the roof, but the brother was crucified and something like a flame was coming from his mouth.
He then woke up disturbed and recounted to the groom and the villagers the dream, expressing his certainty that his brother had died, noting exactly the hour and the minute.
In the morning he announced it also to the adjutant of the mountain artillery battery.
Two days later, as he was heading to Sorovic, he saw the messenger bringing a telegram towards him.
— 'Mr. Veterinarian!' he said to him. 'You have a telegram…'
— 'Yes, I know…' he replied with absolute certainty.
'Open it! My brother has died…'
Indeed, the messenger opening the telegram saw that the news was true.
— 'But how did you know?' he said in amazement.
At that very moment, Dimosthenous fainted and fell from his horse.
In this case the telepathy in representational and symbolic form is evident.
Within the framework, however, the sleeping man's brain also introduced the following:
a) Instead of another physician he associated his acquaintance, the professor,

b) Regarding the operation, he thought immediately as a physician that 'it could not be performed in the contaminated atmosphere of the village house at Peteresko and preferred the clean air of the roof'.
It is well known that in a dream even the most absurd things seem natural to us, because the control of judgment functions defectively.
c) Death was symbolized by crucifixion.
d) The flame further symbolized the soul departing from the mouth of the dying.

The well-known pathologist Nikolaos Lorandos, when about to sit his doctoral examinations in Paris under Professor Debrenne, had a strong feeling that he would be examined on 'Bronchopneumonia', and the certainty continued even when he stood before the professor.
Indeed, the professor asked him about 'Bronchopneumonia'.
We have already mentioned in previous issues of the 'Chronicles' similar cases of the physicians I. Papakonstantinidis, P. Panagiotou and K. Papathanasiou, chief physicians.

In this case, however, Lorandos, as is also apparent from other psychic phenomena submitted in the January 1928 issue of 'Psychical Research', had strong intuitive properties and unconsciously suggested to his professor the topic he was vividly thinking about.

In 1919, the ambassador Andreas Papadiamantopoulos, suffering from softening of the brain due to arteriosclerosis, had already been bedridden for five months, sometimes recognizing or not recognizing his family members, denying their identity, claiming he had never married and was never capable of conversation.
Suddenly, however, one day he told the nurse attending him that he could see an officer outside the door and she should go and open it, as he was bringing some items for them.
The nurse, thinking it was a hallucination, calmed him down and hastened to recount what was said to his family, because due to the world war, the patient had abandoned his furniture in Rukhchuk.

About twenty minutes later there was a knock at the door and Captain Sninas appeared, who informed the son who opened the door that he came on behalf of General Mazarakis to bring their furniture, which had arrived from Rukhchuk in a Bulgarian railway wagon.
Indeed, the furniture was received and the wagon remained with the state.
In this case we are dealing with telepathy between the captain accompanying the furniture — who was naturally thinking intensely about Papadiamantopoulos — and the patient.
What is particularly interesting is that the state of the brain appears to have no significance for the functioning of telepathy.
Indeed, if the telepathic phenomenon were a simple wireless telegraph between two brains, the slightest disturbance or alteration of that organ would make it impossible.
In the present case, we have a brain altered by arteriosclerosis, with all its condition, and the telepathy was conducted effectively.
Of course, there is the reservation that since the patient also had lucid intervals, perhaps the phenomenon was conducted during one of them.
In any case, however, no one can deny the importance of the observation.
Alexandros Kolaxizelis, a medical student in 1928, submitted to the writer the following of his own experience:
He saw on 14 November 1922, while in his homeland of Mytilene, his aunt who was ill in Germany, and who as he knew was returning to Greece, lying in a ship's cabin, dying in a state of asphyxia.
When he woke in tears, because he was particularly fond of her, he recounted the dream to his parents.
That afternoon they received a telegram that indeed his aunt was returning and that she had died during the night during the crossing between Piraeus and Chios.
When the ship arrived, however, they noticed the face of the deceased was dark from the asphyxial state that caused the agony of the death throes, exactly as in the dream.

In this case, it is one of representational telepathy, in which what is remarkable is the fidelity of the images not only of the cabin and the death agony, but also of the asphyxial condition of the dying woman.

articles.title: The 'Grey Shadow' That Heralds Our Death
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: How certain mediums predict our death — The psychic 'rays' that penetrate matter — Clairvoyance and Rhabdomancy.

content.value: How certain mediums predict our death — The psychic 'rays' that penetrate matter — Clairvoyance and Rhabdomancy.
In the previous issue we saw the abilities of certain mediums to see without the aid of sight, but through other parts of the body, such as the forehead, the ears, or the stomach.
Upon this clairvoyant ability of certain mediums are also based the observed phenomena of predicting deaths, which are wrongly attributed to fate.
For, as we shall see later, fate does not exist in the details of life.
There are then mediums who, upon seeing certain individuals, foretell their imminent death.
The manner of their perception differs among them and is sometimes entirely indefinite.
Mediums, for the most part, perceive impending death unconsciously, without being able to express how.

There are, however, exceptions, such as the wife of Veraïnde, professor of Physics at the University of Oslo (Norway), who also visited Athens during the 3rd International Congress of Psychophysiology in 1930.
Mrs Veraïnde usually sees a grey shadow near the person about to die.
Once she sees it, within three days at most, that person will be dead.
The following episode is characteristic:
Once in Oslo this lady was conversing with the director of a bank.
At a certain moment a friend of the banker entered — tall, stout, lively, full of energy and vigour — who stayed and chatted with them for a few minutes.
When he left, the director, turning to Mrs Veraïnde, who had been watching the newcomer with curiosity, said to her:
— 'Why did you look at my friend so curiously? If you saw the grey shadow in him too, you are mistaken… He does not seem to have any desire to die…'
— 'And yet I saw it…' replied Mrs Veraïnde with distress, while the banker shrugged his shoulders with a laugh.
Well, that very evening, the friend was struck by acute appendicitis and, urgently transferred to a hospital, underwent an operation and died.
In the meantime it was found that the case was one of intestinal gangrene.

The explanation of this case is that Mrs Veraïnde subconsciously perceived the gangrene in the visitor's organism and her imagination represented the impression with the symbol she had become accustomed to: the grey shadow.
Conclusion: From this phenomenon it is inferred that various substances emit some form of radiation, since instruments have already been constructed that record it.
The minimal oscillations of their magnetic needle, however, cannot even be compared with the vigorous movements of the divining rod, especially over flowing waters.
These radiations, affecting the subconscious of mediums, produce the movements of the rod.

When, however, crimes are discovered by rhabdomancy, lost individuals are found, or illnesses are diagnosed, it appears rather to be a direct and straightforward perception by the subconscious of the dowsers, as Professor W. Barrett also concluded when examining the phenomenon on behalf of the British Society for Psychical Research.
The best method for conducting such experiments is the following:
We place inside a box a number of identical envelopes, each containing inside opaque paper a word, a letter, a number, etc.
From these we take one at random so that telepathy is excluded, and give it to the medium.
If the medium finds the content, it is a case of Clairvoyance.
A similar phenomenon was demonstrated at the Society for Psychical Research, to some degree, by the mediums Kassandra and Manglidou.
The great Crookes, one day while experimenting, happened to place his finger on a newspaper that was behind him and asked the medium to read the word that was beneath it.
And the medium succeeded, finding the word.
In this experiment it is of interest that the force emitted by the medium returns, as we said, to inform it of what is the matter.
Here is the main point to which we must draw our attention, because it shows us that some invisible force emitted from the body of the mediums acts in a conscious manner.
The phenomena of Sophia Veraïnde — namely that she sees near persons about to die, even if they appear apparently in perfect health, a kind of grey shadow — deserve to occupy us further.
The grey shadow is a kind of dim outline she sees emitted from the body more or less time before death.
Its shape is similar to the body, the hue a diffuse grey, but so indistinct that no features can be distinguished.
Vigorous movements are often observed in it, somewhat like those of a candle flame but without brightness.

This shadow is perceived only in adults and not in children, in whom only a small spherical luminous shape appears, following the body at a distance of 1–2 metres, and with which the medium has the impression it is connected.
At the moment of death the shadow merges with the body and then only an intense flash is visible.
The medium has seen this shadow since childhood.
She remembers that at the age of two and a third years she saw it in her grandfather, but now she sees it more indistinctly.
In her grandfather the vision was so vivid that when she sat on his knees she confused it with him, thinking the phenomenon entirely natural, yet was puzzled when advised not to speak of such things.

When the dying person suffers from an acute illness or dies from an accident, the shadow appears a few days or weeks before death, whereas if the person is suffering from a chronic condition or is about to be bedridden, the shadow may be visible for years on end.
In the next issue we shall see some interesting related incidents of the appearance of the 'grey shadow'.

articles.title: The Human Mystery
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: —What is man in nature? —Do we too have superhuman abilities? —Man, a lesser god. —What is the purpose of psychical research?

content.value: What are psychic phenomena? Many often wonder.
Do they really exist or not? Is there evidence that they occur? What purpose does their investigation serve?

Their existence has been proven by the experience of centuries, and in Greece the 'Society for Psychical Research' has tried to explain and record the reality of the phenomena through the mediums it used.
But what are these strange phenomena and how is their investigation conducted?
The history of psychic phenomena
Psychic phenomena were always observed, even in the most distant ages of human experience, and formed the basis of the first religions.

Primitive religions, like those of today's 'savages', based on the belief that the phenomena originated from the spirits of the dead, which following entreaties or magical ceremonies communicated with the living through certain privileged individuals — the priests or sorcerers — individuals of a particular temperament,
whom today we call mediums or intuitives.
Little by little, as civilization evolved, this conception was modified.
It was supposed that it was not the spirits of the dead who communicate, but the Divine manifesting itself through mediums.
Thus the first oracles were established with the Pythiai as intermediaries.

The Pythiai were powerful mediums who lived in complete abstinence and asceticism, like the Indian Yogis and Tibetan Lamas, in order to reduce their consciousness and bring about the manifestation of psychic phenomena.
Today, mediums no longer need such ordeals to produce phenomena.
Hypnosis or ecstasy suffice.
Nevertheless, the religious asceticism that disturbs the natural equilibrium produces very powerful psychic manifestations.
The miracles of the prophets and saints, as recounted with exaggerations in the Synaxaria,
are not merely the product of fanatical imagination.
The psychophysiologist studies them with curiosity and recognizes
the principal psychic phenomena: Telepathy, Clairvoyance, Psychometry, Ectoplasm, Telepsychia, Levitation, and the like.
The Pythiai, before delivering oracles, fasted, drank an intoxicating drink, the Pythion,
chewed laurel leaves and also underwent the effect of carbon dioxide vapours.
Today's shamans of primitive peoples, before invoking the supposedly dead spirits, drink intoxicating beverages and dance to exhaustion.
Psychic phenomena thus accompany man together with his intellect and give the impression that they are due to the spirits of the dead.
During the 19th century, however, certain telekinetic phenomena produced by two powerful mediums,
the Fox sisters in Hydesville, America,
and believed to confirm communication with spirits, spread widely among Anglo-Saxon peoples and later throughout the world.
The cold and critical scientific spirit, however, was not satisfied with the insufficient verification of the phenomena and the explanation given by the allegedly spirits.
In 1859, the 'Dialectical Society' of London appointed a committee of well-known scientists,
tasked with examining psychic phenomena and expressing an opinion.
Despite the general belief that the committee would reject the phenomena, after months of careful experiments the conclusion was absolutely confirmatory:
'Yes! The phenomena exist, although we cannot provide any explanation.'
This caused a stir in the press and among the intellectuals of the time, who, influenced by materialist theories, categorically refused to believe in anything supernatural.
They even requested the formation of a new committee with the participation of the famous physicist and chemist Crookes, the most brilliant scientific mind of the era.
This committee, led by Crookes, examined the best-known mediums of the time for nine months.
Despite the initial conviction that the psychic phenomena would prove to be products of sleight of hand, the committee's decision burst like a bomb.
Its report is still preserved as a monument of scientific argumentation and clarity.
'Despite the astonishment of our senses and reason, we are obliged to emphasize that psychic phenomena exist, although we are unable to provide any explanation.'
Professional deniers, who limit themselves to denying without experimental data, should show us our errors or propose better experimental methods.
The uproar this time exceeded all limits.
The scientists of the committee suffered mockery, insults, and abuse.
Crookes was even accused of being in love with one of the best mediums of the time, Florence Cook,
with whom he conducted his most extraordinary individual experiments.
However, the conclusions of these two committees led to the establishment, in 1882, of the British 'Society for Psychical Research',
with the participation of eminent scientists, with the aim of rigorous scientific examination of psychic phenomena.
Today, corresponding societies exist in all civilized places and, through the continuous training of new mediums, try to confirm and publicize the experimental truth of psychic phenomena.
What is man in nature?
There were two views: that man is only matter, or that he consists of matter and soul (energy).
Today, following the discovery of radioactivity, it has been proven that the entire universe consists of electrons — that is, electricity — a form of energy.
Man, therefore, consists in part of the same creative energy in the form of electrons and is part of universal harmony.
This creative energy, the basis of all things, must necessarily have superhuman properties, which exist even in the last, if only latent, pebble.
Proof of this is the study of radioactive substances (Radium, Uranium, Thallium, Actinium, etc.), which undergo spontaneous dematerialization.
