articles.title: The Psychodynamism of the Dying
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: One of the forms of Telepathy is the Telekinetic, which proves that the psychodynamism of the dying has mechanical force at the moment of death.

content.value: In 1925, at the home of university professor Mr Bartholomaios Gkyzis on Charilaou Trikoupi Street, an astonishing phenomenon occurred.
One night, while the misses Dina, Kalomoira and Marika Kovaios were dining and the professor was absent, Dina locked the dining room door twice.
Suddenly, at around 11:30, the locked door opened by itself, causing terror and the flight of the women to the upper floor.
Half an hour later, their brother announced to them the death of their father at that same hour.
There are two interpretations: either it was caused by the psychodynamism of the dying father, or by the powerful medium Dina.
In the first, the psychodynamism retains its reason and acquires superhuman properties to notify the children.
In the second, Dina's subconscious through psychodynamic emission caused the opening of the closed door.
This is experimental proof of the soul as a dynamic agent acting consciously.
Professor Mr Gkyzis noted that the phenomenon recurred during Dina's stay at his house.
The painter Georgios Chatzopoulos saw in his dream the old servant 'Maria the Mooress', and subsequently found her alive.
It is excluded that her voice reached his bedroom, so it was a case of telepathy.
Antonios Rouben heard in his sleep his sick mother asking him to take care of his siblings.
The door of the room was indeed open in the morning, while it had been locked.
A telegram confirmed the death of his mother at the same hour as the incident.

articles.title: Do the Dead Communicate with the Living?
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Do the dead communicate with the living? Summary. Psychic waves. Prognosis of the assassination of Prime Minister Th. Deligiannis.

content.value:
Do the dead communicate with the living?
Summary
Psychic waves.
 Prognosis of the assassination of Prime Minister Th. Deligiannis.

Psychic waves
A truly strange form of Telepathy, which superficially might be judged as the appearance of a dead person, sometimes manages to save an innocent person and sometimes to expose their killer. The case is as follows:
On 28 August 1925, near the village of Kapoutsides in Thessaloniki, the corpse of a shepherd, S. Pargianas, was found, faithfully and movingly guarded by his four dogs. With great difficulty the authorities managed to drive them away so that the forensic examination could be conducted and subsequently the burial.

Following summary investigations, two other shepherds were arrested as suspects, against whom there were uncertain indications. A few days after the murder, however, the brother of the murdered Pargianas saw him in a dream, covered in blood, telling him:
—'Listen! I was not killed by those who were arrested, but by S. Servos or Arsakis together with a Vlach shepherd, and they took twenty napoleons I had… But my dogs gave them a proper thrashing and it will be a long time before the bites they gave them heal…'

The simple shepherd recounted the dream the following day to his friends, but hesitated to submit it officially. Finally, persuaded by those who heard him, he submitted it to the examining magistrate Mr Mountakis, who had undertaken the investigation of the crime.

To his great surprise, as soon as he pressed Servos for explanations, the latter confessed to the crime. On his body, moreover, were found the still open bites of the dogs. Thus, thanks to the dream, the innocent detainees were saved and the case was clarified.
In this case, the most plausible and credible explanation is that the dead man appeared in the dream of his sleeping brother in order to save the innocent and expose the real culprit.

If we accept this explanation, a logical question immediately arises: if the dead can communicate so easily with the living, why do so many crimes remain unsolved and so many guilty parties unpunished? Why should entire families suffer, because they cannot learn where the deceased relative hid his will or his money?
There have been and still are secrets in society upon which the honour and life of individuals depend. And yet they were never revealed and perhaps never will be.

The matter would be so simple, if the murdered person came immediately in the sleep of his relatives or of the examining magistrate to reveal the culprit, or if the deceased specified where he had hidden his money or his will. With this premise, life would be simplified astonishingly: no one would dare commit a crime or fraud, and courts would be almost superfluous.

Furthermore, if some immaterial spiritual life after death is superior to the pedestrian material life we live — as the saintly spiritualists affirm — then there should be no mysteries or problems in the sciences. Medicine would have no incurable diseases, Chemistry and Physics would solve all their problems and regulate social life. All hidden treasures would be found, and absolute clarity about immortality would allow man to comfortably arrange his affairs at the hour of death and to bid farewell to his loved ones without pain, in order to pass to a better life.

Unfortunately, none of this happens. Courts continue to be necessary, and sometimes, due to lack of evidence, release criminals or send innocent people to the gallows. Medicine has incurable diseases, and Chemistry and Physics try to solve mysteries with needles, while hidden treasures appear only by coincidence. The approach of death sends shivers through the living…

It is therefore more natural to seek the other explanation: the brother of Pargianas happens to be a medium, to have intuitive abilities, and while sleeping, receives telepathically the thoughts of the murderer.

Indeed, tormented either by remorse or by the vivid memory of the murder, it is natural that in moments of half-sleep he thinks intensely about the scene, especially if he still had some memories of it that gave him no rest. The bites of the dogs, which had not healed, pained him. Thus his thoughts, emitting psychic waves, found the suitable receiver in the murdered man's brother.

The subconscious of the sleeping man — the 'antenna' or 'frame', let us say, of the living radio receiver — transmitted the impression to the brain. The brain, however, as we said before, is a tremendously symbolic organ. Every impression it receives from the 'antenna' it dresses in different 'clothes', presenting it in symbolic form. Here it presented the impression in the clothes of the murdered brother, who came to expose as his own not only the thoughts of the murderer but also his discomfort from the bites of the dogs.
This explanation at least is more natural and logical.

Prognosis of the assassination of Prime Minister Th. Deligiannis
The public prosecutor Mr A. Riganakos submitted in December 1926 the following incident, which was published in 'Psychical Research':
—A lady of my acquaintance, of a hysterical nature and about 22 years old, frequently suffered from attacks during which she fell into a lethargic state. A few days before the assassination of Prime Minister Deligiannis, while she was in this state, she began to cry out:
—'Oh! Oh! What wreaths… what crowds!'
When asked, she replied that she could see the funeral of Deligiannis on Stadiou Street, with the image it presented — the crowd and the abundance of wreaths on the day of the actual funeral.

At the time I paid no attention to what she was saying and only as a joke recommended to the then Prefect of Laconia Mr Antonios Papapan, not to request a transfer from Gytheion, because the Prime Minister would die and there would not be time to abolish certain Prefectures and Courts of First Instance, as he intended to do.

In this case too, as in the Pargianas dream, the hysterical young woman, gripped by an attack and in a state of reduced consciousness, served as receiver to the thoughts of the murderer Kostagkerakaris, who was planning the deed. The brain, as before, symbolized the impression in a simpler form.
In the next issue we shall see the explanation of this case.

articles.title: Auditory Hallucination
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: Danger of experimenting with hysterical mediums. Transfer of impressions to P. Nirvanas. Cases of auditory hallucination. Case of telepathy through hypnosis.

content.value:
Summary
Danger of experimenting with hysterical mediums


Transfer of impressions to P. Nirvanas


Cases of auditory hallucination


Case of telepathy through hypnosis

Danger of experimenting with hysterical mediums
In the previous issue we saw the case of telepathy recounted by public prosecutor Mr Riganakos, concerning a woman who in a lethargic state saw the funeral of Prime Minister Deligiannis.

In this case we have telepathy through a hysterical medium.
Many, including scientists, consider that only hysterical individuals can have psychic phenomena. This is incorrect. There are perfectly healthy mediums, with no trace of hysteria. Simply, for psychic phenomena to be produced, such as Telepathy, Clairvoyance or Psychometry, some reduction of consciousness is required, often during sleep.
This reduction can also occur during normal sleep, which is why many psychic phenomena appear as dreams.

It does not mean that all those who have such dreams are hysterical. In parallel, the same reduction is caused by hypnosis, certain intoxicating beverages (e.g. from the Mexican cactus 'Peyotl'), chronic heart or lung diseases, the death agony, etc.
Hysteria is one of the causes of reduction of consciousness, due to the attacks it produces. But this does not mean that all psychic phenomena are due to hysteria, nor that all individuals are hysterical. On the contrary, I would personally not recommend to amateur experimenters to work with hysterical individuals, because:
The experiments are agonizing


The attacks and intense images terrify those present


There is a risk of unpleasant experiences that will discourage experimenters


Hysteria abnormally irritates the imagination and nerves of the subjects, distorts their perception and develops obsessions and eccentricities, creating a feverish world of mythomania, self-accusation, and anonymous letter-writing.

Transfer of impressions to P. Nirvanas
The late Pavlos Nirvanas, chief physician of the Navy, dreamed one morning that he was called to the home of the then ill minister Chatzikyriakos. He went immediately and found him suffering from the same illness he had seen in his dream.
Initially he believed that his mother, or someone else, had informed the sailor who carried the message and his imagination had staged the dream. When, however, he learned that the sailor had given no information at all, he realized that it was a simple telepathic transfer of the thought of the sick Chatzikyriakos, who, thinking about his illness, prompted him to come, while Nirvanas was in a state of partial reduction of consciousness (sleep).
This phenomenon prompted Nirvanas to become a member of the Greek Society for Psychical Research and a member of its board of directors until his death.

Case of auditory hallucination
On 8 June 1925, the natural scientist and pharmacist Ch. Zouraris in Heraklion, Crete was getting ready for work. As he was picking up his jacket, he heard clearly a voice saying:
—'Don't put that on!'
This prompted him to put on a work garment he had not worn for three years. Shortly afterwards a message arrived from his employee Tasos Karfopoulos, informing him that he was ill and would not be coming to work.
Zouraris knew nothing about the employee's illness and it was impossible for him to have predicted it. This is a case of telepathic transfer of the sick employee's thought to his superior, which produced an auditory hallucination.

Case of telepathy through hypnosis
The distinguished ophthalmologist Nikolaos Mavris, a specialist in hypnosis, collaborated with the physician Rentzou, who had strong intuitive abilities.
One day Rentzou said to him:
 —'I feel that something unpleasant is happening that concerns you. If I hypnotize you, I will be able to tell you.'
After being hypnotized, she told him that his father in Egypt was ill. After waking from the hypnosis, she remembered nothing. A few days later, a letter from his father confirmed the illness.
The same physician, as a soldier during the occupation of Smyrna, wanted to experimentally transmit thoughts to his friend in the same city. He concentrated, focused his thought, and repeated the idea that she should come for help. After an hour, the friend appeared, influenced by the telepathic impression.

articles.title: Intuitive Individuals (MEDIUMS)
articles.author: Άγγελος Τανάγρας
articles.excerpt: An examination of the superhuman properties of the human body through the phenomena of telepathy, clairvoyance and psychometry, with emphasis on the concept of the medium as a living radium.

content.value: Our Superhuman Properties
What do we mean by 'superhuman property'? If we study the sublimation of Radium (alpha, beta, gamma rays), we see that these rays penetrate matter and see through it, like Röntgen rays and X-rays.

As Physics teaches, all bodies can become radioactive under certain conditions (light, heat, X-rays, etc.). The human body is matter, made of electrons, and the proof that it has superhuman properties in a latent state is the mediums, who can be considered living Radia.

As soon as certain conditions affect the organism and disturb its normal balance — such as sleep, hypnosis, chronic cardiac and pulmonary diseases, the death agony, or fanatical belief — truly superhuman phenomena are manifested.

Telepathy, Clairvoyance, Psychometry, Ectoplasm, Telekinesis, Telepsychia, and Levitation, which we shall describe in sequence in the 'Police Chronicles', are astonishing manifestations of the creative energy that constitutes the animal body and which we all possess in a latent state. We are indeed all lesser Gods.

Another proof of these our superhuman properties is the functioning of the organism and its organs, the marvellous details of nutrition, self-defence, healing, and development, which constitute in part the subconscious of the soul.
The Purpose of Psychical Research
From time immemorial the great questions have been posed by the thinking man: what is he, where does he come from, where does he go.

If we believe in materialism, everything collapses into ruins. The entire marvellous universe, whose smallest detail causes astonishment, is the result of blind coincidence — chaos before, exhaustion after. Look then to enjoy life indifferently.

Since the discovery of radioactivity, which proved that matter is condensed energy, materialism has in essence collapsed, and psychic phenomena deliver the final blow to its work. For they prove experimentally the existence in the human body of some other factor that does not fall under our senses and has superhuman manifestations.

This factor, through mediums, guesses the thoughts of others (Telepathy), sees through matter (Clairvoyance), moves objects and performs mechanical acts (Telekinesis), dematerializes and reconstitutes matter (Ectoplasm), etc.

Today the existence of the soul and the problem that thousands of years of philosophy tried in vain to solve is proven experimentally through Telekinetic phenomena, and this is the most beautiful gift that this new science can offer to humanity.

Most people, absorbed in the struggle for life, have no idea of the triumphant conquests of the human spirit. Theologians cannot even imagine them. Mainstream science has to be dragged by force to the experiments. Everyone, however, once they attend, is transformed from Saul into Paul. With one opening of the eyes to the new light they become enthusiastic heralds of it.

Simply look at the names of those who served on the board of directors of the Society for Psychical Research: Katsaras, Voreas, Liviiratos, Bensis, Nirvanas, Filadelfeus. The cream of Greek science and intellect.

Is there a finer victory than this for a new science in its first steps? And the donation of the daughter of the late national benefactor Emm. Benakis, Mrs Alexandra K. Choremi, who undertook to secure the continuity of the work at the twenty-fifth anniversary, completes this victory.
Psychophysiology as a science

Psychophysiology, which was shaped into a new branch of science over the last 70 years thanks to the courageous action of the British Society for Psychical Research and continues not in steps but in leaps, has an unshakeable foundation: demonstrative experiment.
Since the time of Bacon, the scientific world has ceased to love fine words. It wants deeds and without demonstrative experiment believes nothing. Herein precisely lies the value of Psychophysiology.

Every assertion of it, thanks to the mediums, is demonstrated. Today the Greek Society for Psychical Research, though it has worked for 25 years without a penny and without any support, can prove even to the most skeptical the greatest phenomena of psychophysiology, the superhuman properties of the human soul: Telepathy, Clairvoyance, Psychometry.
The experimentation is so easy and fascinating that in a very short time it will pleasurably occupy every intellectual person, even if the discovery of mediums is not difficult.

Some imagine that mediums are rare, like comets. Well, no! According to my personal experience, ninety-five per cent of people are mediums to a greater or lesser degree. Some of these are naturally extremely powerful, but most of the rest, if suitably trained, can develop these marvellous properties. In India and Tibet this training is applied on a large scale.

We can easily discover mediums as follows: we select individuals who have noted psychic phenomena in their lives, such as telepathy with their relatives, fulfilled dreams, premonitions, etc. We ask them to clasp the fingers of both hands. When we count one, two, three, they will not be able to open them. Indeed, some of them could not open them.

These individuals, susceptible to suggestion, are the mediums sought, suitable for our experiments. One of them can try hypnosis according to the instructions in Volume 2 (1926) of 'Psychical Research', written for the training of experimenters.

In this case we emphasize one point: there is a prejudice that hypnosis is harmful. Not at all. The best and oldest mediums of the Society have iron-clad health. I have however seen weak, pale, lymphatic individuals who had been practising the profession of medium for years and today have doubled or tripled in volume.

This, however, is understood when the prospective medium is not a sickly individual. Then even the slightest thing can cause harm — a little walking, more food, etc. For this reason it is essential, before beginning systematic experiments with an individual, that they be examined by qualified physicians. Sickly organisms, particularly those with organic heart damage and intensely excessive temperaments, are useless and dangerous for the experimenter.

articles.title: Ten Years of the Society for Psychical Research - 1933
articles.excerpt: The Society for Psychical Research completes ten years of activity and celebrates at Parnassos. Angelos Tanagras presents the Society's work and the goals for the next decade.

image.alt: Ten Years of the Society for Psychical Research

content.value: Ten years have already elapsed since the Society for Psychical Research was founded. This entire decade passed with sustained efforts, initially to combat the various petty prejudices and the scientific community's mistrust toward psychic phenomena. The results of these efforts were extraordinary.

Thanks to the invaluable support of Alexandra Choremi, née Benaki, and the great dedication to the Society's scientific work by its director Mr. Angelos Tanagras, the Society for Psychical Research is today known in all centres of psychical research.
Under the motto 'believe, but also investigate,' the Society for Psychical Research managed, within a decade, to present a very progressive body of work.
All this work, which justifies a grand and solemn celebration of its tenth anniversary, is being marked today morning, modestly, in the great hall of Parnassos.

The Society for Psychical Research's work is already more or less known to the public. The fact that the Society's breakthrough began with a single medium, Mrs. Konstantina Nikolaidou, and today presents ten-odd scientific mediums — the ladies and misses Zakynthinou, Karyoti, Nikolaidou, Kassandra, Pavlatou, Pallada, Kikiou, etc. — is, we believe, a rather great achievement alongside its other great accomplishments.

With the moral standing gained from its achievements and with the support it now anticipates from the State and from distinguished Greeks, it will continue its work so as to become not only a scientific but also a nationally precious asset. It will work to achieve the following goals:
The establishment of a postgraduate training institute for university graduates.
The intensive continuation of long-distance telepathy experiments.
The dissemination of the experimental truth of psychic phenomena through experiments and lectures, and
The recognition of Athens as a centre of psychical experiments in the Balkans.
All these topics will be developed by Mr. Angelos Tanagras at today's gathering at 'Parnassos'. The achievements of the decade will be presented there in full detail.

articles.title: Tanagras Denounces Laïla Hanoum as a False Clairvoyant - 1936
articles.excerpt: Angelos Tanagras denounces the Romanian Laïla Hanoum as a false clairvoyant. She responds by accusing him of professional rivalry and claims internationally recognised status as a clairvoyant.

image.alt: Tanagras's Denunciation — Laïla Hanoum as a False Clairvoyant

content.value: Mr. Tanagras denounces Laïla Hanoum as a false clairvoyant — the prophetess's reply.
On behalf of the President of the Society for Psychical Research, the following announcement was issued yesterday:

The Society for Psychical Research draws the public's attention to a Romanian false clairvoyant operating under the name Leila Hanoum. Having failed in her attempt to obtain certification from the Society, she installed herself in a large hotel and began cleverly advertising herself in the press in order to deceive the gullible.
The Society immediately notified the General Security Directorate and the Ministries of the Interior and of Health so that measures could be taken against her.

Mr. Tanagras, to whom we turned following the above announcement, informed us that the so-called 'Tefren Laïla,' who presents herself with so many titles, is not a medium and that her prophecies have nothing to do with the investigation of psychic phenomena, nor should they be believed.

Laïla may well possess certain exceptional psychic abilities, but this does not mean she has the power to make prophecies or to know events before they occur. It appears that, according to Mr. Tanagras, 'Tefren Laïla' seeks to exploit the credulity of the naive for profit, as is the case in most instances with various itinerant false prophets who create unwarranted publicity around their names.

Laïla, as Mr. Tanagras tells us, had previously appeared in Greece during the Pangalos dictatorship and caused considerable controversy at the expense of the gullible, always for a fee. It is therefore necessary, according to the president of the Psychophysiological Society, to put an end to this exploitation of the public by various shrewd individuals who travel the world enriching themselves at the expense of the naive.

This could be prevented in Greece if the bill long since submitted by the Society for Psychical Research were to be voted upon — a bill that would not permit anyone to act as a medium in Greece, and even less as a prophet, without first sitting an examination before the Society for Psychical Research, which is the sole competent body to assess the psychic qualities of individuals, to certify what faculties each medium possesses, and what services they may offer the public.
WHAT MRS. LAÏLA REPLIES
Mr. Krankel, secretary to Mrs. Laïla, who had been informed of Mr. Tanagras's statements, responded as follows:

Mrs. Laïla greatly regrets that Mr. Tanagras speaks in such a tone out of a spirit of professional rivalry. Mrs. Laïla is a qualified professor of the Occult Sciences, practising scientifically in the art of 'clairvoyance' with achievements that have secured her an international reputation. Under these circumstances, she naturally cannot accept any comparison with Mr. Tanagras, and even less to be judged by Mr. Tanagras, whom she considers far inferior to herself in the field of Psychical Sciences.

Moreover, despite the strong objections of the Society for Psychical Research, Mrs. Laïla obtained a licence from the relevant competent ministry, and with this authorisation worked in Athens a few days ago as a 'clairvoyant.'
There is no doubt that this unexpected episode — which no 'medium' had foreseen — will have a sequel equally interesting as its beginning.

articles.title: Epidemic of False Mediums - The Suspicious Spirits That Fondle Women 1934
articles.author: G. Kasimatis
articles.excerpt: G. Kasimatis satirises the 'epidemic' of false mediums in Athens. Angelos Tanagras and the Society for Psychical Research request police intervention to protect the public.

image.alt: Epidemic of False Mediums in Athens

content.value: Spirits in danger! Until now we knew that the Athenian spirit was in crisis — witness the glorification of tedium in Athenian theatrical productions. Now, however, the spirits themselves are in crisis. And as is well known, spirits, the incorporeal world, spiritualist gatherings, and spiritualist experiments now play an important role across the ever-widening strata of Athenian society.

If there are naive or practical people who want to abolish spirits from grammar — and surely supporters of this simplification will have great difficulty passing university examinations, where their papers will flounder and ultimately fail, caught between the smooth and the rough breathing mark.
Spirits not only continue to exist in our grammar, but have above all entered our daily life. There is an entire world that believes in spirits.

This preamble was necessary to understand the gravity of the matter. When spirits hold such importance, imagine what the alarm must be when they are threatened. And they are threatened! Mr. Tanagras, the great and indisputable patriarch of spiritualism, is sending out an S.O.S.! Save our souls — or rather, save the spirits.

But let us see: what dreadful enemy threatens the spirits? Simply put, the science of psychic phenomena, as represented by the Society for Psychical Research, whose president is Mr. Tanagras and whose members include scientists and men of letters such as Th. Voreas, M. Katsaras, Sp. Livieratos, V. Bensis, Mr. P. Nirvanas, etc.

Yet the field has now acquired its own charlatans. For charlatans — these fathers and gymnasiarchs of Spiritualism — to exist, naive people must exist as a prerequisite. And this species, as is well known, is plentiful in the world.

Young working-class girls wanting to find out from the false mediums whether their beloved would marry them, widows wishing to hear news from their late husbands, young ladies from the middle class and even from the aristocracy coming for answers to various problems in their lives, men who suspect their wives of unfaithfulness, and women with suspicions about their husbands.
Others, to learn from the mouths of these prophets what the future holds or whether their business affairs will prosper.

In the homes of the false mediums, all manner of people from every social class would come and go to purchase their prophecies. How else, for the thousandth time, could one prove that clever people live at the expense of the credulous? Human stupidity, as the philosopher said, knows no bounds.

In this instance it filled the pockets of the false mediums, who, thanks to the expansion of their clientele, were obliged to rent suitable premises in which to receive their naive clients in a more imposing setting. A classic example is such a medium, Evangelia Skotidaki in Piraeus, who began receiving clients from the Cretan neighbourhood of Piraeus and managed to establish herself on Sokratous Street, one of the most central and prosperous thoroughfares, in order to receive a more affluent clientele.

Her income increased to a degree that permitted her this luxury. It was this woman who gave the Society for Psychical Research grounds to draw the Ministry of the Interior's attention to the activities of the 'false mediums,' whose claim to foretell the future and to practise spiritualism constitutes outright fraud for the exploitation of the public, and to request police intervention. The police acted and succeeded in halting the operations of these post-Christ prophets.
Since then, the Ministry of the Interior has not stopped receiving applications from various mediums requesting a certificate of professional practice as a 'medium.'

That is to say, this charlatanism is so lucrative that its initiates wish to become professional mediums so as to be able to operate freely. Meanwhile, the false mediums have been joined by false hypnotists and false physicians. Business, you see, is taking on vast proportions, and the spirits are multiplying like the sand of the sea. But regarding the mysteries and the sorceries of these prophets, and the scandals occurring in the world today, we shall continue tomorrow.

articles.title: The Phenomena of Agrinio — Tanagras's Explanation 1957
articles.excerpt: Angelos Tanagras visits Aetolia-Acarnania to investigate the strange phenomena at Monastiraki Xyromero and attributes the events to a powerful medium.

image.alt: The phenomena of Agrinio — Tanagras's Explanation (1957)

content.value: AGRINIO, 21 October — The president of the Society for Psychical Research, Mr. Angelos Tanagras, arrived in our city, coming from Amfilochia. The purpose of his visit to Aetolia-Acarnania was to investigate the strange phenomena in the village of Monastiraki Xyromero. For the benefit of the readers of 'Chronographos,' I shall describe these phenomena.
Several years ago, the village schoolmaster of Monastiraki passed away.

With his death, many strange things began to occur in the house of his widow. Plates and glasses were smashed, beds and tables were hurled about, furniture was broken to pieces, and so on. The superstitious villagers attributed these phenomena to the schoolmaster's return as a revenant, and for this reason they burned down the unfortunate widow's house.
However, even after the destruction of the house, these strange phenomena recurred in the new house to which the schoolmaster's family had moved.

The villagers began to grow afraid, and the village priest began to read prayers and exorcisms against the demon. But beyond these measures, they exhumed the deceased and burned him with boiling oil! And yet again 'the demon' did not abandon the schoolmaster's house. Only two years ago did these events cease, when the schoolmaster's widow herself passed away.
Mr. Tanagras, in explaining these events, attributed them to a powerful medium — that is, to the deceased widow. For this reason he did not wish to conduct any related experiments.

Of the schoolmaster's family, only two members are still alive: his son, who has been in America for many years — indeed, since before the phenomena appeared — and his daughter, who lives in Monastiraki but who, according to Mr. Tanagras, is not a powerful medium.
Mr. Tanagras entrusted the monitoring of the daughter to the local physician, with the request that he notify him immediately should the slightest phenomenon appear.
From the house, Mr. Tanagras collected several pieces of destroyed furniture, cut in a most peculiar manner, which he wishes to send to the Institut Métapsychique in Paris.
At the request of many scientists, Mr. Tanagras delivered a lecture at the 'Attikon' cinema. The hall was packed to capacity with scientists and intellectuals of our city.

Mr. Tanagras began by outlining the development of humanity across the centuries, until scholars reached the conclusion that a human being consists not only of flesh and bone, and that the cold slab of the tomb is not everything — but that within each person there exists a 'little butterfly,' upon whose golden wings the hopes of mankind are bound.

He then spoke at length about telepathy, telekinesis, and precognition, citing relevant examples. With an inspired turn of phrase he arrived at the irrefutable doctrine of the existence of God and the immortality of the soul. Vigorous applause greeted his words.
Mr. Tanagras asked the scientists present to form a group in Agrinio as well, one that would collaborate with the Society for Psychical Research and report any phenomena that might occur.
Those present signed a commemorative record on the occasion of the lecture, which was taken by Mr. Tanagras.
Mr. Tanagras departed for Messolonghi, where — according to reports — there is a medium with whom he intends to conduct various experiments.

articles.title: Categories of Psychic Phenomena Under Science - 1931
articles.excerpt: Angelos Tanagras divides 'psychic phenomena' into three categories: suggestion/auto-suggestion, telekinetic-household phenomena, and post-mortem apparitions, with references to international research and spiritualism.

image.alt: Categories of psychic phenomena under science (1931)

content.value: WHAT MR. TANAGRAS SAYS
As for the other phenomena, we must divide them into three categories.

First: a proportion which certainly stems from individual or collective suggestion. This proportion falls within the domain of applied science. It has been observed that in such phenomena, auto-suggestion invariably plays a very important role. In the grip of fear, a troubled imagination sees non-existent things as real.
And those who undergo — naturally and unconsciously — the influence of suggestion, afterwards recount as witnessed things they merely imagined.

Without questioning the good faith of such witnesses, therefore, one must make a small allowance in weighing their testimonies. One must accept from the outset that of what they have recounted, some details are somewhat exaggerated, magnified, blown out of proportion, while others are perhaps altogether inaccurate.
However, for a scientist to determine precisely the proportion attributable in all likelihood to auto-suggestion, he would need to examine the witnesses himself.
The second category encompasses the phenomena observed in the house: the mysterious sounds, the moving of furniture, the opening of doors, and so forth.
All these phenomena fall within the psychical sciences, which today engage scientists of international standing, professors at leading European universities, and scholars of worldwide renown.
These phenomena are very frequently observed and have been studied thoroughly by those engaged in Psychical Research.
These phenomena can be produced by living subjects, but are often also produced by subjects who have died.

They are attributable, according to the generally accepted conclusions of what the psychical sciences have demonstrated, to the electromagnetic radiation of the human body, to the outflow of an electromagnetic fluid known as the 'electrolotron.'

Those who attended the sessions of the International Congress of Psychical Sciences, which was held last year in Athens and in which the most eminent scientists and scholars from the entire world participated, will have heard numerous similar cases announced and presented in the light of science.
APPARITIONS
The third and final category encompasses post-mortem apparitions, which are purely spiritualistic phenomena.
On these, the opinions of those engaged in psychical research are divided.

Most, however, accept the existence of spiritualist phenomena, which they observe and study in specialised spiritualist séances, with the assistance of world-renowned 'mediums' endowed with exceptional psychic faculties.
Beyond scientists, great writers also accept spiritualist phenomena and occupy themselves with their study.

Among others is the famous storyteller Conan Doyle, who recently published much-discussed books, such as 'The New Revelation' and others, in which he recounts astounding instances of spirit apparitions which he personally witnessed and verified.

Likewise, our own Polyvios Dimitrakopoulos had occupied himself with spiritualist phenomena during the last years of his life and left writings on the subject which, regrettably, remain unpublished to this day.
Mr. Angelos Tanagras, in response to the questions we put to him regarding the mysterious phenomena and the post-mortem apparitions that have occupied us these last few days, answered as follows:
'The question you put to me concerns the most important point of "psychophysiology," before which research has come to a halt, undecided.

'And this, because even if it were proven that all traditions concerning revenants are true and are not due to hallucinations caused by suggestion, the matter — the contested matter of immortality in question — would still not be resolved.'
'We may therefore imagine, independently of immortality, that there is something real in the traditions concerning revenants.'
'I shall mention a few things by which you may form your own opinion.'
